---
title: "Introduction"
Author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
format: html
theme: cosmo
editor: visual
bibliography: references.bib
csl: acta-diabetologica.csl
---

The global prevalence of type 2 diabetes (T2D) and related complications continues to rise, indicating that existing prevention and treatment strategies are insufficient to slow down disease progression [@saeedi2019; @ahlqvist2018]. This may partly reflect that T2D is a more complex and heterogeneous disease than previously assumed, and that a diagnosis based only on glucose levels may not cover underlying pathophysiological drivers. Data-driven phenotyping has begun to assess this complexity, by identifying distinct subgroups with different pathological characteristics and risk-trajectories for diabetes-related complications [@ahlqvist2018; @misra2023; @li2024]. Emerging evidence suggests that this heterogeneity is already present among individuals with prediabetes, with an opportunity for more targeted prevention before the onset of type 2 diabetes. [@wagner2026; @onthoni2025; @wagner2021]. Traditionally, risk stratification in prediabetes has relied on glycemic states, such as impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance, arising from a combination of insulin resistance and declining beta-cell function [@echouffo-tcheugui2021]. While glucose patterns during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) support our understanding of risk trajectories among individuals with prediabetes, they provide limited insights into more complex metabolic traits. Among these, body fat distribution and particularly visceral adipose tissue (VAT) and subcutaneous adipose tissue (SAT) has emerged as an important metabolic dimension. Indeed, body fat distribution represents a metabolic trait that is already present and differentiated among individuals in the prediabetic state [@philipsen2015; @borel2015]. This is underscored by recent, data-driven phenotyping, identifying subgroups with different characteristics, including diverse patterns of body fat distribution [@wagner2026; @stroebel2025].

While fat distribution captures important aspects of metabolic risk, postprandial hormonal responses represent another dimension of metabolic heterogeneity that is not well covered in prediabetes phenotyping. The incretin hormones glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) have an important impact on the postprandial insulin response and glucose regulation [@nauck1986]. While GLP-1 lowers blood glucose through both increased insulin secretion and glucagon suppression, GIP primarily stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion. This makes GLP-1 a key regulator of postprandial glucose regulation, demonstrated by the success of GLP-1 based therapies in treating type 2 diabetes and obesity [@holst2024]. Contrary to the glucose-lowering mechanisms of GLP-1 and GIP, glucagon increases glucose levels by stimulating glycogenolysis in the liver and thus, adequate suppression of glucagon during glucose intake is essential to regulate glucose metabolism [@ahrén2015]. The interplay between incretin secretion and glucagon suppression among individuals with impaired glucose metabolism is complex, underscored by conflicting findings in the literature. Incretin secretion has been found to be decreased [@laakso2008; @muscelli2006; @toft-nielsen2001], normal [@vollmer2008; @ahren1997] or even increased [@chong2022; @færch2008] among individuals with impaired glucose metabolism compared to individuals with normal glucose regulation. Furthermore, glucagon suppression has been suggested to be associated to insulin resistance, but also that individuals with prediabetes and screen-detected type 2 diabetes exhibit poor early but normal late glucagon suppression, whereas individuals with high insulin sensitivity showed higher early but lower late glucagon suppression [@færch2016; @wagner2017].

Whether these hormonal response patterns during glucose ingestion are associated with distinct patterns of body fat distribution among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes remains to be established. Therefore, the aim of this study was to identify metabolic subgroups among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes using k-means clustering of OGTT-derived glucose and hormonal responses, and to quantify the associations between these subgroups and measures of body fat distribution.
